briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the daily briefing by the Climate Change Spokesperson for April 8, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Escalating Extreme Climate Threats and Human Society's Vulnerability
sections.content: Today's major domestic and international news clearly demonstrates the direct and severe threats that climate change poses to our lives. It is predicted that extreme weather events combining 'heatwaves and droughts' will increase by more than five times by the end of this century [1, 4], and wildfires are now occurring in areas where they previously did not, presenting a new fear [2]. These climate changes are causing health crises through sea-level rise [135], and are having widespread impacts across ecosystems, such as altering specific greenhouse gases in ways scientists did not anticipate [124]. The warning that the impacts of climate change will extend beyond the current generation to affect the lives of great-grandchildren [129] once again reminds us of the urgency of the crisis we face.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Spread of Domestic Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Amidst these threats, various efforts are actively underway domestically to respond to climate change and move towards a carbon-neutral society. The Chungbuk Institute of Health and Environment has activated a mosquito surveillance system in response to climate change [3, 13], and the Korea Rural Community Corporation is strengthening its adaptation capabilities by operating water management councils to proactively respond to droughts and securing water resources [9, 15, 16]. Regional and technology-based efforts are also noteworthy, such as Yeosu City's preparation for the 'UNFCCC Climate Week' [5, 11] and the Jeonnam Agricultural Research and Extension Services' opening of an 'Advanced Vertical Farming Research Center' [7]. Furthermore, Gwangmyeong City's support for the manufacturing sector's transition to carbon neutrality [21, 22, 47, 67] and Gyeongbuk Province's carbon neutrality consulting for SMEs [25, 26, 35, 40] are promoting decarbonization in the industrial sector, and Woori Bank is concretizing its 'NEXT ESG' strategy to achieve 2050 carbon neutrality [33, 38, 45, 54, 64]. Tree-planting activities across the country [30, 42, 50, 59] and Geoje City's expansion of the carbon neutrality points system [24, 46, 51] demonstrate the importance of practicing carbon neutrality through citizen participation.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Policy Recommendations
sections.content: As the limitations of fossil fuel dependence become clear [72, 79], accelerating the transition to renewable energy is emerging as an even more critical task. While Portugal has demonstrated a successful case by meeting 80% of its first-quarter electricity demand with renewable energy [74], domestically, efforts to expand renewable energy are continuing, with Wonju City providing local government funding for renewable energy housing support projects [86, 101, 105, 116] and the South Chungcheong Provincial Council institutionalizing resident-participatory renewable energy profit sharing [85, 91, 92, 95, 104, 106]. The energy crisis originating from the Middle East emphasizes the need for the government to accelerate the expansion of renewable energy's share to over 20% [96], and innovation in the power grid is also essential as the share of renewable energy generation increases [98]. In particular, the analysis that up to 4.5 GW of renewable energy can be secured through the expansion of residential solar power [103, 118] suggests the potential of distributed energy systems.

sections.title: ⚠️ Obstacles to Climate Action and International Challenges
sections.content: However, significant obstacles still exist in responding to climate change, and international solidarity and domestic policy improvements are urgently needed to overcome them. The case of ExxonMobil hacking a climate activist [8] raises awareness about unethical attempts to hinder climate action, and the criticism that Korea's renewable energy expansion targets fall short of international standards and that energy companies have significant influence [80] demonstrates the need for transparency and independence in the policy-making process. The criticism that the 11th Basic Plan for Long-term Electricity Supply and Demand is inconsistent with IPCC pathways and that power, oil, and gas companies are leading the discussions [100] emphasizes the importance of strong government will and stakeholder coordination to achieve climate goals. Meanwhile, the tendency for ESG investors to prioritize health over climate change [119] suggests that the climate crisis is complexly linked to various social issues, indicating the need for a holistic approach.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted not only the severity of climate threats but also the multifaceted efforts to overcome them, along with the realistic challenges we face. This is a time when stronger and more consistent climate action is required for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
